assistant_message.content: Keep the PM retinoid lane, but make it sustainable instead of irritating. Fix first: Retinol serum appears to be your main PM retinoid step, so keep it low-frequency and do not stack acids or benzoyl peroxide on the same nights; Barrier cream should sit right after Retinol serum on treatment nights so the routine has a real recovery step instead of another active. Current products: Retinol serum: partial match. Keep it as the main PM active, but cap it at 2-3 nights a week; Barrier cream: good match. Keep it as the recovery moisturizer right after Retinol serum. This week: add SPF50 every morning; keep Retinol serum to 2-3 nights a week and follow with Barrier cream.

suggested_chips.label: Tell me more about my skin
What issues does my routine have?
Anything I should watch out for?

current_strengths: You already have a cleanser + moisturizer backbone, so this can be improved without rebuilding the whole routine.
Retinol serum already gives you one clear PM treatment lane.
You already have a daytime sunscreen step in place, so the job is mostly cadence and tolerability.

priority_findings.title: Retinol serum appears to be your main PM retinoid step, so keep it low-frequency and do not stack acids or benzoyl peroxide on the same nights.
priority_findings.detail: Retinol serum appears to be your main PM retinoid step, so keep it low-frequency and do not stack acids or benzoyl peroxide on the same nights.

priority_findings.title: Barrier cream should sit right after Retinol serum on treatment nights so the routine has a real recovery step instead of another active.
priority_findings.detail: Barrier cream should sit right after Retinol serum on treatment nights so the routine has a real recovery step instead of another active.

target_state: Keep the PM retinoid lane, but make it sustainable instead of irritating.
core_principles: Keep only one strong-active lane at a time.
Stabilize the current steps before escalating.
Retinoid nights should be paired with moisturizer and a slower cadence.

am_plan.purpose: Keep the cleanse step simple and non-irritating
Keep barrier support in the morning
Make the existing sunscreen a fixed daily step

pm_plan.purpose: Keep the evening routine simple at the start
Make the retinoid lane tolerable before deciding to intensify
Leave enough recovery support after the active step

keep: Retinol serum: partial match. Keep it as the main PM active, but cap it at 2-3 nights a week.
Barrier cream: good match. Keep it as the recovery moisturizer right after Retinol serum.
SPF50: good match. Keep it as the fixed AM protection step.
Gentle cleanser: good match. Keep it only if it cleans without leaving the skin tight.
Vitamin C serum: partial match. Keep it in the current AM slot for now.

first_4_weeks: Week 1: lock the current routine and stop escalating it.
Week 2-3: keep the retinoid to 2-3 nights per week and watch for stinging, redness, or peeling.
Week 4: only consider another new active if the routine stays fully calm.
week_8_12_expectation: By weeks 8-12, the win should be better tolerance and more complete daytime protection, not aggressive escalation.

ui_card_v1.headline: Keep the PM retinoid lane, but make it sustainable instead of irritating.
key_points: Retinol serum appears to be your main PM retinoid step, so keep it low-frequency and do not stack acids or benzoyl peroxide on the same nights.
Barrier cream should sit right after Retinol serum on treatment nights so the routine has a real recovery step instead of another active.
avoid_now: If retinoid use causes persistent stinging, redness, or peeling, pause it and go back to cleanser + moisturizer only.
ui_card_v1.next_checkin: Week 1: lock the current routine and stop escalating it.

safety_notes: If retinoid use causes persistent stinging, redness, or peeling, pause it and go back to cleanser + moisturizer only.
